main_sections.title: The Case of the Fifth Word
main_sections.prose_summary:
The story is adapted from Donald J. Sobol's Encyclopedia Brown series and centres on Leroy Brown, an exceptionally gifted eighth-grader nicknamed 'Encyclopedia' because of his vast, encyclopaedic knowledge. His father, Chief Brown, is the head of the Idaville Police Department, and their usual practice is for the father to share unsolved cases at the dinner table, where Leroy quietly solves them before the meal ends. The story opens on a Tuesday evening when Chief Brown is visibly troubled. He explains that Tim Nolan, a man long suspected of involvement in a million-dollar jewellery heist from the Diamond Mart five years earlier, has died of a stroke. During the robbery, one of two masked men—believed to be Nolan and his accomplice Daniel Davenport—had his mask slip, but the clerk was not certain enough to testify. No stolen jewellery was ever found, and Davenport disappeared after the crime. That morning, officers had searched Nolan's property—a small palm-tree nursery—and found nothing.

The twist lies in the document found clipped to Nolan's dying will: a sheet from a desk calendar bearing Davenport's name and address, and four cryptic words—'Nom Utes Sweden Hurts'. Chief Brown and Mrs. Brown try to decode the words independently. Mrs. Brown identifies their literal meanings (nominative, an American Indian tribe, a European country, and the verb hurts) but cannot piece together a hidden message. Leroy, however, silently analyses the code. He asks one perfectly targeted question: Is there a young fir tree in Nolan's nursery? When his father confirms there is one on the south side, Leroy reveals that each word is a day of the week with the letters 'd-a-y' removed—Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday—and the unwritten fifth word (the hiding place) is 'Fir', standing for Friday. The stolen jewellery is discovered inside a twenty-gallon jug of earth in which the young fir tree grows. The story celebrates lateral thinking, the power of pattern recognition, and the quiet modesty of true intelligence—Leroy never boasts, choosing instead to blend in with ordinary boys.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the story to find out how the boy wonder solves a case that has been troubling his father. (Introductory reading task with pre-reading activities: I. Solve the crossword puzzle with anagram clues. II. Match different meanings of 'case' with sentences.)

question_bank.stem: Solve the crossword puzzle given below with the clues in the form of anagrams.
Match the different meanings of 'case' in Column 2 with the correct usage in the sentences in Column 1.
question_bank.table:
Column 1|Column 2
1. Lalit said that he could not sing but that was not the case as he sang melodiously later.|(i) writing format
2. The lawyer was waiting for the case to be taken up in court.|(ii) container
3. My pencil case is a gift from my grandmother.|(iii) situation
4. The subject of a formal letter is written in title case.|(iv) matter

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the following statements with suitable reasons. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: Leroy's nickname was Encyclopedia and everyone called him by that name because ______________.
At the dinner table on Tuesday night, Chief Brown stared at his cream-of-mushroom soup as ______________.
Encyclopedia sat quietly at the dinner table because ______________.
Nolan put the will on the kitchen table because ______________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do you think Chief Brown would need Leroy's help to solve this case? If yes, why? If no, why not?
A fact is something that can be proven true or false. An opinion is a belief, feeling, or judgement. Identify which of the following statements are facts or opinions.

question_bank.stem: Encyclopedia's father was the Chief of Police.
Everyone thought that Chief Brown must be the smartest police chief in the country.
An encyclopedia is a book or set of books filled with facts from A to Z.
Leroy's friends said that he was like a library and computer rolled into one, and more user-friendly.
Two masked men held up the Diamond Mart on Sixth Avenue.
Nolan and Davenport had met while both were in prison in South Carolina.
Chief Brown's hunch was that Davenport and Nolan decided to hide the loot until things cooled down.
Nolan wrote a four-word code to tell Davenport where he had hidden the stolen jewellery.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the table by choosing the character traits of Leroy from the box. There is an extra word you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Match textual evidence with character traits: cleverness / knowledgeable / keen listener / gentle / humble.
question_bank.table:
Textual Evidence|Trait
1. He read more books than anyone in Idaville, and he never forgot a fact.|knowledgeable
2. Encyclopedia never spoke of the help he gave his father. He didn't want to seem different from other boys.|__
3. Encyclopedia sat quietly. He knew his mother and father were discussing the case for his benefit.|__
4. Usually, he needed to ask only one question to solve a case before dessert.|__

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: Chief Brown would have liked to tell everyone about his only child. But who would believe him? Who would believe that the best detective alive was an eighth grader? So, he said nothing. Encyclopedia never spoke of the help he gave his father. He didn't want to seem different from other boys…

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence with a suitable reason. Chief Brown's dilemma can be called unique because____________.

What can be inferred about why Encyclopedia never spoke of the help he gave his father? A. He is shy and lacks confidence in his abilities to share his insights. B. He wants to avoid the jealousy others might feel about his skills. C. He desires to blend with the other boys to prevent seeming different. D. He is embarrassed about being smarter than most adults around him.

The parents choose not to call their son 'Encyclopedia'. Select the reason that is false. A. For them he is more than his unique abilities; he is their child, with a personal identity. B. Calling him by his real name reflects their affection and the desire to acknowledge him as an ordinary boy. C. They want him to be remembered as different from the others around.
What does the writer mean by the fact that Leroy was 'stuck with' the name Encyclopedia?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extract and answer the questions that follow. Extract 2: Chief Brown studied the four words: Nom Utes Sweden Hurts. He shook his head and passed the notebook to Mrs. Brown again. 'Can you figure it out?' 'Nom is a shortening of nominative…' She lifted her gaze to Encyclopedia and shook her head. 'I can't figure it out,' she confessed.

question_bank.stem: What does Chief Brown's reaction of shaking his head suggest about his understanding of the four words? A. He is confident that the words are a clue. B. He is confused, unable to make sense of the words. C. He believes the words are random and meaningless. D. He thinks the words are unrelated to the case.
Identify whether the given statement is true or false. Mrs. Brown explained the actual meanings of the individual words rather than decoding the hidden message behind them.
What does Mrs. Brown's inability to decode the words suggest about the message?

Complete the following sentence suitably. The purpose of Encyclopedia's reminder in the last line of the extract was to _________________. (refocus the discussion on the context of the crime / suggest that the coded message was meant for Davenport / imply that the message was likely a clue about the location of the stolen jewellery)
options: refocus the discussion on the context of the crime
suggest that the coded message was meant for Davenport
imply that the message was likely a clue about the location of the stolen jewellery

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Nolan and Davenport were very close. Support this statement with evidence from the text.
Why did Davenport disappear right after the jewellery hold-up? What might his plans have been if Nolan had not died?
What does Mrs. Brown's interest in the case tell us about her?
Explain why Chief Brown was proud of his son.
Why were Chief Brown's suspicions regarding Nolan and Davenport justified, even when there was a lack of concrete proof?
Analyse the role of the four-word coded message in the story.

Write a character sketch on Leroy with the help of the character traits table given in 'Let us discuss' section. You may begin it like this: Encyclopedia was a knowledgeable eighth grader who never forgot a fact that he read.

listening.transcript_text:
HOST: Hello everyone! In today's episode, we'll be discussing some simple but effective ways to improve our observation skills. Being observant isn't just about seeing, it's about truly noticing details in the world around us. Whether it's in daily life or in the workplace, observation skills can help us become more aware, make better decisions, and even improve memory.
First, slow down and take your time. Rushing through tasks often leads to missed details. When you take time to focus on what's in front of you, you're more likely to notice features you might otherwise overlook. When you pause and observe, the details become clearer.
Another effective method is to engage all your senses. Observation isn't just about seeing; it's also about hearing, smelling, touching, and even tasting. The more senses you engage, the more information you absorb.
A third tip is to ask questions. Curious people tend to be better observers because they actively try to understand what they're noticing. Asking questions forces your brain to think critically about what you're observing.
Next, take notes or make sketches. Writing down what you observe can help reinforce details in your memory. Even quick sketches of an object or place can strengthen your understanding of its features.
Finally, it's important to practise mindfulness. Mindfulness helps you stay present in the moment and fully engage with your surroundings. Start incorporating these techniques into your daily routine, and soon you'll be observing things you never noticed before!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a podcast about ways of improving observation skills. As you listen, mark the statements 1–4 as true or false.

question_bank.stem: Observation skills help improve your common sense.
Rushing through tasks helps you focus better and observe more.
Taking notes or making sketches helps with what you observe.
Incorporating tips on observation skills will soon lead to success.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will once again listen to the podcast. As you listen, complete the given sentences by filling in the blanks with one to three exact words you hear.

question_bank.stem: It is important to slow down and ___________________ to notice several aspects around you more clearly.
The more senses like hearing and smelling you use, the more ___________________ you absorb.
People who are ___________________ tend to be better observers because they seek to understand what they notice.
When you make a note of things, it helps reinforce the ________________ in your memory.
Practising mindfulness helps a person stay ___________________ with their surroundings.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following words from the text loudly with the help of pronunciation guide given in brackets: jewellery (jool-ree), clerk (claak), dessert (dih-zuht). Now, practise reading the following words: January, sour, pizza, Wednesday, bowl, buffet, pronunciation.

Work in groups of five or six. Conduct a group discussion on the topic, 'The Interesting Aspects of a Mystery Story'. The moderator initiates the discussion, speakers take turns using polite expressions to agree or disagree, speakers may interrupt by raising their hands, the moderator ensures equal participation, and the moderator sums up and thanks members. Use the given phrases for turn-taking, agreement, and disagreement.

Now, as a member of the Art and Craft Club, write a report on an 'Inter-school Art Exhibition' organised by your school. Include the necessary details. (Sample report on inter-school football tournament is provided showing format: title with byline, paragraph 1: what/who/when/where, paragraphs 2-3: detailed description with sequence markers, paragraph 4: prize-giving ceremony and remarks.)
Match the phrasal verbs from the text given in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. You may refer to a dictionary. Then fill in the blanks with the phrasal verbs.

question_bank.stem: Match phrasal verbs: put on / cooled down / turned up / make of / figure out / got away with — with their meanings: appeared/been discovered / solve a problem / became normal or calmed / wear / escaped without being punished / have an idea or understanding of something.
question_bank.table:
Phrasal Verb|Meaning
put on|(i) appeared, been discovered
cooled down|(ii) solve a problem
turned up|(iii) became normal or calmed
make of|(iv) wear (clothes, hat, etc.)
figure out|(v) escaped without being punished
got away with|(vi) have an idea or understanding of something

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks with the phrasal verbs: A. Finally, some important evidence about the case has ___. B. After I ___, I understood that I had made a big mistake. C. I couldn't ___ why he was angry with me. D. He ___ his special coat and went to his friend's birthday party. E. Ravi forgets to do his homework but he ___ it as teacher didn't ask for it. F. I do not know what to ___ his behaviour.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks in the following sentences by choosing the correct word pairs from the box: week/weak, dying/dyeing, fore/four, peace/piece, break/brake.

question_bank.stem: After running for 50 kilometres last _________________, I felt _________________ for two days.
When the committee met yesterday, they brought _________________ local cases to the _________________.
While learning to drive, Rina used too much force on the _________________, causing the car to _________________ down.
He has composed a beautiful _________________ of music in order to bring _________________ to thousands of people.
She was busy _________________ her hair not caring about the _________________ flowers in her garden.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Leroy solved the mystery of the missing fifth word with his critical thinking abilities. Work in pairs to solve the puzzles given in the illustrations and infer what they say. (Visual word puzzles: e.g., 'TOP SECRET' = top secret; 'It stand It' = understand; 'AGED AGED AGED' = middle aged; 'Try uu GO' = try it out; '2 It' = too good; 'ONCE R' = once in a while; multiple 'sense' = common sense; '1 M I L L I O N R O A D S' = million roads/possibilities.)

Spoonerism is a mistake made when speaking where the first sounds of the words in a phrase are exchanged. Find the correct order for the following spoonerisms: 1. knowing sits 2. it's roaring with pain 3. plaster man 4. blarm wanket 5. tars and crucks 6. shake a tower 7. bead a rook 8. mardon me padam.

question_bank.stem: Find the correct (original) phrases for each spoonerism: 1. knowing sits 2. it's roaring with pain 3. plaster man 4. blarm wanket 5. tars and crucks 6. shake a tower 7. bead a rook 8. mardon me padam.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the sentences from the text and study the highlighted relative/subordinating conjunctions. Fill in the blanks with the words in the box (where/why/who/though/which/when/because/whose/if/how) to complete a paragraph about detective stories. There are two extra words you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Detective stories are exciting, keeping readers guessing (i) ____________ the real culprit is. They take place in settings (ii) ____________ clues are hidden carefully. (iii) ____________ the mystery seems tricky, a clever detective always finds answers. Readers often wonder (iv) ____________ criminals leave small but important clues. The challenge is to decide (v) ____________ suspect is guilty. (vi) ____________ the truth is revealed, everything makes sense. But one may wonder (vii) ____________ they could have solved it themselves. These stories are fun (viii) ____________ they make readers think till the very end.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Combine the following pairs of sentences using the word given in brackets. (Example given: 'We gave him the signal that he was waiting for.')

question_bank.stem: We went to a place. The incident occurred there. (where)
Teacher said she would retire soon. She is now nearly sixty. (who)
It was the day. Half the class was absent. (when)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the direct speech sentences from the text (interrogative sentences). Learn the rules for transforming direct to indirect (reported) speech: tense changes, pronoun changes, time and place changes, steps for Wh- questions and Yes/No questions. Now, follow these steps to transform the sentences given as examples from direct speech to indirect speech.

question_bank.stem: (iv) Mrs. Brown asked Chief Brown ________________________________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Rewrite the following sentences in Reported Speech.

question_bank.stem: 'Why did you stop talking to me?' asked Rohan. Rohan questioned __________.
'Did you enjoy the family gathering yesterday?' asked my aunt. My aunt asked ______.
'When will you visit us again?' asked my grandmother. My grandmother inquired ______.
Madavi said to Ravi, 'Will you come with me to meet our old teacher tomorrow?' Madavi asked Ravi ______.
My friend said, 'What gift are you planning to give your brother?' My friend inquired ______.
'Shall I carry these notebooks to the staffroom, Ma'am?' asked Veenu. Veenu asked her teacher ______.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the conversation between Aditi and Rahul. Complete the following paragraph by transforming the dialogue into indirect speech.

question_bank.stem: Aditi asked Rahul 1. ___. Rahul replied that he had noticed a man wearing a blue jacket drop it off. Aditi asked Rahul 2. ___. Rahul replied that it had been around 6.45 p.m. Aditi further inquired 3. ___. Rahul said that he had worn a hood, so he couldn't see his face.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs. Read the words and observe the images of optical illusions. Check if your answers match — these are optical illusions which may trick the eye and make you think that you can see two different things. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

Read the steps to play a language game — 'Name the Mystery Object'. Divide the class into teams. Each team chooses an object and prepares three hints. Teams take turns to name the object. Points: first hint = 20, second hint = 10, third hint = 5. One example provided with three hints about a blackboard.
Visit the library and look for an encyclopedia. What is it? Check how it is different from a dictionary. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.
A thesaurus is a reference book or digital tool that provides a collection of words with similar meanings. Choose any five words and find out their alternative words from the thesaurus.

main_sections.title: The Magic Brush of Dreams
main_sections.poem_text:
Go and gather wild beans, Gopi,
Go and fetch some rice.
Go and pluck the mangoes
And bring home something nice.

Gopi sits beneath the banyan,
A twig is in her hand.
She sits there drawing pictures,
Pictures in the sand.

The winds blow by and sweeps away
The pictures in the sand.
But on a stone there sits a man,
A brush is in his hand.

He looks around. He calls to Gopi.
"Come here!" he whispers. "Hush!
We mustn't let the village know
About this magic brush."

He slips the brush into her hand
And tells her to be sure,
"Paint not for the wealthy ones,
But only for the poor."

"Did you gather wild beans, Gopi?
Did you fetch some rice?
Did you pluck the mangoes
And bring home something nice?"

"No beans, no rice, no mangoes!"
Gopi grins and runs inside.
She paints a bowl of khichdi
and waits,
Until the paint has dried.

"The bowl is full of khichdi, Gopi!"
Into the house the villagers streamed
The young and old all wish to see
The magic brush of dreams.

She paints a muffler for a farmer,
A plough for another man,
A shawl for a grandmother,
And for a girl, a fan.

And soon the news spreads far and wide,
Through forests, fields, and plains,
Until the Zamindar himself
Hears of these magic gains.

"I order you to paint for me,
A fortress tall and grand.
Paint treasures, jewels, and riches rare,
Enough to rule this land."

Gopi bows and shakes her head.
"My lord, I can't comply.
I swore to use this magic brush
To help the needy by."

The Zamindar roars and stamps his foot.
He bellows to his men,
"Seize this brush and seize the girl.
Throw her in the pen!"

Now Gopi waits inside the cell,
Upon a cold mud floor.
Until the Zamindar returns
And shouts through the door.

He holds the brush. He thunders loud,
"Paint my fortress tall!
Paint gold and rubies, emeralds bright,
And you shall leave this hall!"

That night the Zamindar lies in bed,
Dreaming of his gold,
While Gopi paints a winding road,
A horse both strong and bold.

The road leads out of prison gates.
The horse, she swiftly urges
Gopi leaps on and gallops fast,
Through fields and many villages.

"It's Gopi! It's Gopi! She's back again!"
Say the villagers, merry and kind.
But Gopi keeps on painting still,
With the Zamindar's men following
behind.

She paints a mighty river wide,
Its currents fast and deep.
The Zamindar and all his men
Stop short and cannot leap.

"Now should I paint a beast
To leap and chase you through?
Stop this chase or it will feast
On you, and you and you!"

The Zamindar cries, "No, no, no more!
I'll leave, I'll go away!"
He turns and flees; his men behind
Dare not choose to stay.

Now Gopi paints for the village folk,
A feast of sweets and tea.
A courtyard bright, a band to play,
And songs of victory.

Adapted from a Folk Tale
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Adapted from a folk tale, 'The Magic Brush of Dreams' is a narrative poem in ballad form, following a predominantly ABCB rhyme scheme that lends it a rhythmic, story-like momentum. The central figure, Gopi, is a young girl who receives a magic paintbrush with the condition that she use it only for the poor. Her defiance of the greedy Zamindar—who imprisons her and demands she paint riches for him—forms the dramatic climax. Gopi's ingenuity and moral courage are the poem's core themes: she paints not weapons but a road, a horse, a river, and the threat of a beast to outwit authority and protect the village. The dominant imagery is sensory and earthy—bowls of khichdi, a winding road, a mighty river—reinforcing the poem's folk-tale roots. The magic brush symbolises the transformative power of art and imagination in service of justice, while the Zamindar's fortress and gold represent greed and oppression. The tone shifts from playful and innocent (Gopi drawing in sand) to tense and confrontational (imprisonment), and finally to triumphant and celebratory (victory feast). Repetition, as in 'On you, and you and you!', builds dramatic tension and reinforces the universality of the threat against the oppressor.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do these activities before we read: I. Work in pairs. Discuss: 1. Mention a magical object you would like to have. Give a reason. 2. What would you draw if you had a magic paint brush? Why? 3. Would you use the magic paint brush for someone else? If yes, why? If no, why not? II. Discuss instances of acts of kindness you have heard, read, or experienced. III. Work in pairs and guess the meanings of the words given below and draw pictures: paintbrush, joy, fortress, feast, fear, winding, twig, bows, drawing, cold. Then arrange the words into categories: Things, Actions, Emotions.
Arrange the events in order of occurrence in the poem. The last one has been marked for you.

question_bank.stem: Arrange in order: 1. Gopi paints a bowl of khichdi which becomes real. 2. The Zamindar orders Gopi to paint treasures for him. 3. Gopi is told to gather food but sits drawing pictures in sand. 4. Gopi returns to the village with the Zamindar chasing her. 5. The Zamindar throws Gopi into prison. 6. Gopi paints a road and horse to escape. 7. Gopi uses the magic brush to paint useful items for villagers. 8. Gopi paints a wide river stopping the Zamindar. 9. A man gives a magic brush to Gopi to paint only for the poor. 10. Gopi scares the Zamindar away and celebrates with the villagers. [10 is last]

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks by choosing the correct answer from the box: triumphant and celebratory / tension and suspense / playful and innocent. The tone of the poem shifts throughout, reflecting the different moods of the story.

question_bank.stem: Initially, the tone is 1. _________________ as Gopi uses her imagination to draw in the sand, and later when the Zamindar enters the story, the tone shifts to one of 2. _________________. In the final part of the poem, the tone becomes 3. _________________ as Gopi escapes, the Zamindar is defeated, and the villagers rejoice.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks by choosing the correct option from within the brackets.

question_bank.stem: The poem mostly follows a simple rhyme scheme, _________________, which adds a rhythmic flow. (ABCB, ABBA, ABAB)
This poem belongs to the _________________ poetry genre, which tells a story through verse. (descriptive, narrative or ballad, free style)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Give examples from the poem for the following explanations.

question_bank.stem: The poem is rich in imagery, creating clear pictures in the reader's mind. For example, ___________________.
What is the effect of repetition in the line—'On you, and you and you!'?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the phrases from the poem in Column 1 with what they symbolise in Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Match: 1. The magic paintbrush / 2. The grand fortress / 3. The river and the beast — with: (i) greed and the desire for wealth / (ii) barriers to protect Gopi from the Zamindar's anger / (iii) the power of art and imagination to change the world.
question_bank.table:
Phrase|Symbolises
The magic paintbrush|(i) greed and the desire for wealth
The grand fortress|(ii) barriers to protect Gopi from the Zamindar's anger
The river and the beast|(iii) the power of art and imagination to change the world

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Identify whether the following statement is true or false. While Gopi uses the magic brush to help others, the Zamindar wants to use it for personal gain. This contrast drives the moral of the story.

question_bank.stem: While Gopi uses the magic brush to help others, the Zamindar wants to use it for personal gain. This contrast drives the moral of the story.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: 'But on a stone there sits a man, / A brush is in his hand. / He looks around. He calls to Gopi. / "Come here!" he whispers. "Hush! / We mustn't let the village know / About this magic brush." / He slips the brush into her hand / And tells her to be sure, / "Paint not for the wealthy ones, / But only for the poor."'

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blank: The man is whispering and telling Gopi to keep the brush a secret reflects his _______________________ (careful and secretive / magical and delightful) nature.
What is the significance of the man slipping the brush into Gopi's hand?
Complete the following sentence with a suitable reason. The most likely reason the man instructs Gopi to 'Paint not for wealthy ones but only for the poor' is because he _______________________.
Select the most appropriate title for the extract: A. The Magic Brush's Secret  B. Gopi's Special Gift.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extract and answer the questions. Extract 2: 'And soon the news spreads far and wide, / Through forests, fields, and plains, / Until the Zamindar himself / Hears of these magic gains. / "I order you to paint for me, / A fortress tall and grand. / Paint treasures, jewels, and riches rare, / Enough to rule this land."'

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence: The news of Gopi's magic brush spreads 'through forests, fields, and plains' because of its _______________________.
Why does the poet refer to things painted by Gopi's paintbrush as 'magic gains'?

Which of the following statements is false? Rectify the false statement. A. Gopi's magic brush draws the attention of the powerful Zamindar. B. The Zamindar requests Gopi to paint a grand fortress and jewels.
What does the Zamindar's demand for a grand fortress and jewels reveal about his character?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Explain why Gopi made the right decision by refusing to paint for the Zamindar.
Gopi threatens to draw a beast to scare away the Zamindar. Justify her action. How would you have dealt with the situation differently?
What is the significance of Gopi celebrating with the villagers at the end of the poem?
Compare Gopi's use of the magic brush to how the Zamindar would have used it.
What can be inferred about Gopi's character based on her refusal to follow the Zamindar's orders?
Justify the appropriateness of the title of the poem.
If you had the magic brush, how would you use it in today's world?

listening.transcript_text:
Speaker (i): If I could have any magical power, I'd choose the ability to predict the future. Imagine being able to see profits and losses before time. I could make better savings and secure my business. Plus, I'd use it to help others make wise financial decisions as well.
Speaker (ii): I think having the power to learn instantly would be amazing! Exams would be easy, and I'd have more time for activities I enjoy, like playing cricket. If I could remember everything, it would make life so much easier!
Speaker (iii): I would want the power of flying. Imagine being able to see the world from up above clearly. I would be able to go from one place to another quickly without using any means of transport. It would be a dream come true.
Speaker (iv): At my age, I'd love to have the power of healing. There are so many aches and pains that come with growing old, but it's not just for me. If I could heal others too, I'd help my family stay healthy and happy.
Speaker (v): If I had a magical power, I'd want control over the weather. We depend so much on the rains. With this power, I could ensure that crops always get the right amount of rain and sunshine.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to five speakers speak about a magical power they wish for. As you listen, match statements 1–7 with speakers (i)–(v). There are two statements you do not need.

question_bank.stem: Match each statement with the correct speaker. Statements: 1. Power of healing ailments / 2. Ability to end poverty in the world / 3. Power to control the weather as per need / 4. Ability to make wise business decisions / 5. Power to predict one's own future clearly / 6. Ability to remember information quickly / 7. Power to move around without using vehicles. Speakers: (i)–(v).
question_bank.table:
Statement|Speaker
Power of healing ailments|__
Ability to end poverty in the world|__ (extra)
Power to control the weather as per need|__
Ability to make wise business decisions|__
Power to predict one's own future clearly|__
Ability to remember information quickly|__
Power to move around without using vehicles|__ (extra)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Intonation is the rise and fall of the pitch of tone while speaking. The 'falling' tone is used in imperative sentences. Read the following sentences from the text with the falling tone: 'Come here!', 'Seize this magic brush…', 'Paint my fortress tall!', 'Go and fetch some rice.'

Work in pairs. Take turns to make requests for the following formal and informal situations. Formal: 1. Request teacher to organise a class picnic. 2. Request teacher for an extra sports period since you missed it last week. 3. Request Activity incharge teacher to include you in the upcoming inter-school event. Informal: 1. Request sibling to help with completion of your project. 2. Request mother to enroll you in a dance class. 3. Request cousin to come over to spend the weekend with you. Use given phrases for formal and informal requests.

Write an imaginative essay on any one of the following. (A sample imaginative essay 'My Life as EcoFlash' and a checklist for what an imaginative essay includes are provided.) 1. Imagine you were a scarf that flew away from a clothesline. Describe the places you travelled to and how you came back. Also write about what you thought was the most valuable experience. 2. Imagine you stumbled upon a magical book in the library. The book transported you to a different time period. Describe your experience and an important learning.
The verb 'draw' has different meanings in different contexts. Match the sentences in Column 1 with their appropriate meanings in Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Match: 1. His speech drew loud applause from the audience. / 2. The event will draw huge crowds. / 3. Plants draw water from the soil. / 4. I reached the station when the train was drawing into the station. / 5. She drew the curtains. / 6. He drew a piece of paper from the folder. — with meanings: (i) opened / (ii) pulled out / (iii) got a reaction / (iv) attract / (v) moving / (vi) absorb.
question_bank.table:
Sentence|Meaning
His speech drew loud applause from the audience.|(i) opened
The event will draw huge crowds from all neighbouring places.|(ii) pulled out
Plants draw water from the soil.|(iii) got a reaction
I reached the station when the train was drawing into the station.|(iv) attract
The room was dark, so she drew the curtains to let the sunlight in.|(v) moving
He drew a piece of paper from the folder.|(vi) absorb

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the crossword. Clues from the poem are given (Across: gallops, surge, whispers, snatch, wealthy, seize; Down: flees, mighty, bellows, comply, streamed, victory).

There are certain expressions that convey a sudden feeling or reaction (interjections). Match the expressions in Column 1 with the emotions or reactions they convey in Column 2. Then create sentences using any five expressions.

Interjection|Emotion

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Have you heard of India's famous magician, P. C. Sorcar Sr.? He is known as one of the 'World's Greatest Magicians' and performed live magic shows internationally. He was awarded Padma Shri on 26 January 1964.

Words can also play tricks. Read the riddles about things that seem to be unreal and find what they refer to: 1. I can fill a room, but I take up no space. I'm often heard, but never seen. What am I? 2. The more you take, the more you leave behind. What am I? 3. I have cities but no houses, I have forests but no trees. What am I? 4. I'm tall when I'm young and short when I'm old, in a dark room, my story is told.

question_bank.stem: Solve the riddles: 1. I can fill a room, but I take up no space. I'm often heard, but never seen. 2. The more you take, the more you leave behind. 3. I have cities but no houses, I have forests but no trees. 4. I'm tall when I'm young and short when I'm old, in a dark room, my story is told.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You are asked to create a magical object. What object would you create? Make a drawing with all its details and mention its special quality on the same sheet. Put up the sheet on the display board.

main_sections.title: Spectacular Wonders
main_sections.prose_summary:
'Spectacular Wonders' is an informational text that describes seven remarkable natural phenomena of India, celebrating the country's extraordinary and diverse landscapes. The text opens with a broad statement about India's mystifying natural phenomena and then profiles each wonder individually.

The Valley of Flowers in Chamoli, Uttarakhand is a UNESCO World Heritage Site containing around 600 exotic flower species, rare wildlife including the snow leopard, and spectacular Himalayan backdrops. The Living Root Bridges of Meghalaya are engineering feats built by indigenous people using intertwined roots of trees, solving the age-old problem of crossing waterways during monsoons; some bridges are 175 feet long and can persist for hundreds of years. The Lonar Crater Lake in Buldhana, Maharashtra, was formed by a meteorite impact thousands of years ago and is the only crater in India formed in basaltic rock, ranking third largest globally; the crater holds a unique saline lake. The Magnetic Hills near Leh, Ladakh create an optical illusion where vehicles appear to roll uphill on the so-called Magnetic Road, caused by the peculiar alignment of surrounding slopes and the horizon. The Glowing Waters of Kerala at Kumbalangi village near Kochi exhibit bioluminescence caused by a high concentration of micro-planktons, making this an eco-tourism destination. The Sundarbans in West Bengal, spread over 1,400 km in the delta of the Ganga, Brahmaputra, and Meghna rivers, is a mangrove forest home to Bengal tigers, estuarine crocodiles, Indian pythons, and diverse bird species. Finally, Chandipur Beach in Balasore, Odisha is known for the sea retreating 2–5 km during ebb tide and returning with high tide, creating the illusion of the sea playing hide-and-seek with visitors.

The text concludes by framing these phenomena as a blend of scientific intrigue and folklore magic, reminding readers of the extraordinary forces shaping our environment.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do these activities before we read: I. Look at the pictures and sort them into natural and man-made wonders. II. Which of these wonders—man-made or natural—are more appealing and why? Share your answers. III. Write down two wonders of nature that have amazed you. Discuss what makes them impressive. IV. Work in groups of four. Complete the web chart with words you associate with the phrase 'Nature's Power'.
Complete the information in the table given below. One example has been done for you.

Valley of Flowers — Chamoli, Uttarakhand — Around 600 species of exotic flowers [example given]. Fill in for: 2. The Living Root Bridges / 3. Lonar Crater Lake — Buldhana, Maharashtra / 4. Magnetic Hills / 5. Glowing Waters / 6. Sundarbans — West Bengal / 7. Chandipur Beach — Balasore, Odisha.)
question_bank.table:
Amazing Natural Phenomena|Location|One Unique Feature

The Living Root Bridges|__|__

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: 'India is home to some of the most mystifying natural phenomena that surprise and spark our imagination.'

question_bank.stem: Replace the underlined phrase with one from the extract. India is the birthplace of several rare species of flora and fauna that thrive in its diverse ecosystems.
What does the phrase 'nature's unusual side' suggest about the phenomena described?

Complete the similarity by choosing the correct option. spark : imagination :: ______________ : ______________. A. inspiration; creativity  B. wonder; amazement  C. effort; reward  D. knowledge; books
Identify whether the following statement is true or false. Some natural phenomena can surprise people.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the given extract and answer the questions. Extract 2: 'Have you seen things disappearing in front of your eyes?…This happens in Odisha. It is a beach in the Balasore district where one can witness the sea disappearing mysteriously—the Chandipur Beach which is known for its unique feature of playing hide-and-seek with its visitors.'

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence: The writer's most likely purpose to introduce information about the Chandipur Beach phenomenon with a question is to ____________________. (engage the reader's curiosity and create a sense of suspense / draw the reader to learn more about the unusual phenomenon / set the tone for the surprising nature of the content that follows)
options: engage the reader's curiosity and create a sense of suspense
draw the reader to learn more about the unusual phenomenon
set the tone for the surprising nature of the content that follows

question_bank.stem: Select the option that is correct for both Assertion (A) and Reason (R). (A): Chandipur Beach in Odisha is known for its unique feature of the sea disappearing. (R): The tides retreat drastically, exposing the Chandipur beach. A. Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A). B. Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A). C. (A) is true, but (R) is false. D. (A) is false, but (R) is true.
What does the term 'unique' suggest about the beach in Balasore? A. very popular  B. one of its kind  C. ancient place  D. far from city
What does the writer want to convey with the use of the phrase 'playing hide-and-seek with its visitors'?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: How are the living root bridges a boon for the local people of Meghalaya?
Why is the Lonar Crater Lake considered a remarkable and rare site?
What might be the writer's purpose of highlighting the awe-inspiring natural wonders of India?
The writer uses descriptive language and introductory questions for some of the natural phenomena. What impact does it have on the readers?
Which of these unusual phenomena did you find the most interesting and why?

listening.transcript_text:
Brother: I liked the pictures Uncle Dileep posted yesterday from the Republic of Peru. Can you guess which was my favourite?
Sister: I know it was the Rainbow Mountain. I say that because it was certainly my favourite.
Brother: Really! I didn't know it was your favourite too. Can you imagine the creativity in Nature? What a marvellous natural phenomenon!
Sister: I could not believe my eyes when I saw that picture. There are so many colours there. Do you know how it's formed?
Brother: Of course! It's actually quite amazing. The mountain gets its colours from layers of minerals like clay, limestone, and sandstone that got deposited millions of years ago.
Sister: Wow, I read that too. I think each colour is due to a different mineral. Like, the green comes from copper, and the red from clay. It's like nature painted the mountain!
Brother: I cannot believe the fact that it was all buried under snow until recently.
Sister: Oh! The melting snow must have made it visible again. That's why it looks so fresh and bright today.
Brother: Those layers of minerals were actually deposited by water from ancient seas and lakes.
Sister: Each colour represents a different time in history. That's why it's called the rainbow mountain.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a brother and sister discuss a natural phenomenon. As you listen, mark the four true statements from 1–6 given below.

question_bank.stem: Mark true or false: 1. The brother was taken aback to see the posted pictures. 2. The brother admitted to not knowing about his sister's favourite picture. 3. The sister stated that the rainbow mountain was like any other mountain. 4. The brother was aware of the reason for the formation of colours on the mountain. 5. The brother was surprised to learn about the mountain being covered with snow till recently. 6. The sister understood nature's role in the beauty of the mountain.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in groups of four. Make a presentation on a place of interest in and around your region that people like to visit. Guidelines: Slides 1–2: choose a popular place. Slides 3–4: location and how to reach. Slides 5–6: attractions, popularity, historical importance. Slides 7–8: weather, local food, customs, language. Slides 9–10: best time to visit and reason for recommendation.

Write a descriptive essay for your school magazine describing anything from nature that attracted your attention. It may be an object, a place, or an event experienced by you. Remember to give a title. Use proper format and content organisation—begin with a title, include introduction (one paragraph), body (two to three paragraphs), and conclusion (one paragraph).
Fill in the blanks with the correct expressions from the text given in the box: came into existence / to no end / in full glory / in disbelief / to name a few / age-old problem.

There are no simple solutions to the _____ of traffic jams. 5. He opened the ancient wooden box and stared at the treasure _____. 6. The garden is _____ in spring with lots of beautiful flowers in bloom.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Binomials are a pair of fixed words joined by 'and' or 'or'. Match the binomials in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. Then use the binomials in sentences of your own.

question_bank.stem: Match: 1. odds and ends / 2. on and off / 3. mix and match / 4. all or nothing / 5. part and parcel / 6. pick and choose / 7. sooner or later / 8. leaps and bounds — with meanings: (i) put different things together / (ii) complete part of or belong to / (iii) choose only the best / (iv) at some time in the future / (v) sometimes, occasionally / (vi) increase or develop very quickly / (vii) small, unimportant things / (viii) something done completely or not at all.
question_bank.table:
Binomial|Meaning
odds and ends|(i) put different things together to get a range of possibilities
on and off|(ii) complete part of or belong to
mix and match|(iii) choose only the best (things, people, etc.)
all or nothing|(iv) at some time in the future
part and parcel|(v) sometimes, occasionally
pick and choose|(vi) increase or develop very quickly
sooner or later|(vii) small, unimportant things
leaps and bounds|(viii) something to be done completely or not at all

question_bank.stem: Use the binomials from Column 1 in sentences of your own.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the highlighted words in sentences from the text ('living', 'striking', 'rugged') — these are participles (verb forms used as adjectives). Present participles end in -ing and describe ongoing actions; past participles usually end in -ed/-d/-t/-en/-n and describe completed actions. Fill in the blanks using present participle or past participle form of the verbs in the box: stand / amaze / carve / scatter / glow.

question_bank.stem: Fill in blanks with correct participle form: 1. The _______________ marble monument looks even more spectacular in the moonlight. (carve) 2. The _______________ view of the Himalayas from Darjeeling attracts visitors. (amaze) 3. The Sun Temple in Konark features intricately _______________ stone wheels and walls. (carve) 4. _______________ at a height of 182 m, the Statue of Unity is dedicated to Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel. (stand) 5. The _______________ islands of the Andaman and Nicobar offer some of the cleanest beaches. (scatter)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the highlighted word 'Crossing' in the sentence from the text — this is a gerund (verb form used as a noun, ending in -ing). Gerunds can serve as subject, complement, object of a verb, object of a preposition, or after certain verbs. Underline the gerunds and participles in the following sentences. Write 'G' for gerunds and 'P' for participles.

question_bank.stem: Identify gerund (G) or participle (P): 1. Dancing is a great way to express emotions. 2. Travelling can broaden your perspective. 3. The falling leaves signalled the arrival of autumn. 4. She enjoys cooking new recipes for her family. 5. Reading helps improve your vocabulary. 6. He watched the shimmering stars in the night sky. 7. Forgetting the keys inside, Kanchan locked the door.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Combine the following pairs of sentences using participles or gerunds. (Example: 'Entering the room, Nitesh saw his friend sitting in the corner.')

question_bank.stem: 2. Smitha walked carelessly. She slipped on the doormat. (participle)
3. Sleep for eight hours. It is good for health. (gerund)
4. Ranjith felt happy about the result. He called his parents. (participle)
5. Father encouraged Meena. Meena joined the course. (gerund)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Man-made Wonders: 1. Read about Kallanai Dam in Tamil Nadu — one of the world's oldest dams, built by the Chola King Karikala around 150 CE. 2. Read about Nalanda in Bihar — the world's first residential university, declared UNESCO heritage site in 1980. Visit https://nalandauniv.edu.in/about-nalanda/history-and-revival/ 3. Read about Hampi in Karnataka — Vittala Temple's 56 SaReGaMa pillars that produce musical notes when tapped. 4. Modern wonders: Bandra-Worli Sea Link, Statue of Unity, Bhupen Hazarika Setu, and others.

Some micro-planktons in the sea are bioluminescent. Have you seen any other life form that has this quality? Identify the insect given below and find out what it is called in your language. Look out for it when you visit a field or a place with a wetland.
